title: P-Adapters: Robustly Extracting Factual Information from Language Models with Diverse Prompts

structured.summary: The paper proposes P-Adapters, lightweight neural modules that adapt natural language prompts into continuous prompts to improve the robustness and accuracy of factual extraction from frozen large language models without requiring additional annotations. Evaluated on the LAMA benchmark across multiple models and out-of-distribution settings, P-Adapters significantly improve precision and consistency compared to baseline prompting methods, offering a practical approach to handle prompt variation in factual knowledge retrieval.

strengths.point: Annotation-free adaptation of natural language prompts
strengths.evidence: P-Adapters are trained end-to-end using only (prompt, object) pairs without requiring relation or subject annotations at training or inference.

strengths.point: Significant improvements in precision and consistency
strengths.evidence: P-Adapters improve precision by 12-26% absolute and consistency by 36-50% absolute over baseline natural language prompts across multiple LLMs and OOD settings.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation on diverse and challenging settings
strengths.evidence: Experiments include in-domain, out-of-distribution prompts, OOD objects, and prompts with typographic errors, demonstrating robustness of the method.

strengths.point: Insightful analysis of subject embedding importance
strengths.evidence: The study shows that preserving or interpolating the original subject entity embedding is critical for performance, highlighting an important architectural consideration.

strengths.point: Comparison with stronger baselines and oracle models
strengths.evidence: P-Adapters perform competitively with mixture-of-experts models that require additional annotations, and oracle models provide upper bounds contextualizing results.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to simple prompt ensembling baselines
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not compare P-Adapters against prompt ensembling methods, which are common annotation-free baselines for improving prompt robustness.

weaknesses.point: No ablation or justification of adapter placement and size
weaknesses.evidence: The choice to insert P-Adapters between embedding and first attention layer is not ablated or justified, nor is adapter capacity varied to assess sensitivity.
The paper omits comparisons to other continuous prompt tuning methods that do not require relation annotations, weakening claims of novelty.

weaknesses.point: Oracle model uses unrealistic gold relation labels at inference
weaknesses.evidence: The oracle MoE model relies on gold relation labels at inference, limiting practical relevance and no exploration of noisy relation prediction is provided.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation metrics and consistency definition are limited or ambiguous
weaknesses.evidence: Only Precision@1 is reported without metrics capturing partial correctness or semantic similarity; consistency metric lacks clarity on prompt pair selection.
Hyperparameters for training P-Adapters and prompt preprocessing steps, especially for OOD prompts with typos, are not detailed.

weaknesses.point: No discussion on scaling to larger LLMs or multi-token object prediction
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments are limited to BERT and RoBERTa sizes; multi-token object prediction is not addressed, limiting generalizability and applicability.

weaknesses.point: No evaluation beyond LAMA or on language/domain variation
weaknesses.evidence: All experiments focus on LAMA relations in English, with no tests on other datasets, languages, or specialized domains.

weaknesses.point: Figures and tables lack statistical significance reporting
weaknesses.evidence: Reported improvements do not include confidence intervals or significance tests, making robustness of gains unclear.
The interpolation mechanism for subject embedding is not clearly explained or formalized, hindering understanding.

questions.question: How does P-Adapters performance compare to simple prompt ensembling methods?
questions.motivation: Prompt ensembling is a strong, annotation-free baseline for robustness; understanding this comparison is critical to contextualize gains.

questions.question: Would P-Adapters remain effective and scalable on much larger language models (e.g., GPT-3 scale)?
questions.motivation: Scaling behavior affects practical adoption and generalizability of the method to state-of-the-art LLMs.

questions.question: Can P-Adapters handle multi-token object predictions and more complex factual queries?
questions.motivation: Many real-world factual relations require multi-token answers; addressing this would broaden applicability.

questions.question: How sensitive is performance to the architectural choices of adapter placement and size?
questions.motivation: Understanding these sensitivities can guide better design and optimization of P-Adapters.

questions.question: How robust are P-Adapters to noisy or incorrect relation predictions in the MoE setting?
questions.motivation: Since oracle MoE uses gold relations, exploring noisy predictions would clarify practical utility.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to simple prompt ensembling baselines. The paper does not compare P-Adapters against prompt ensembling methods, which are common annotation-free baselines for improving prompt robustness
critique_points.point_002: No ablation or justification of adapter placement and size. The choice to insert P-Adapters between embedding and first attention layer is not ablated or justified, nor is adapter capacity varied to assess sensitivity
critique_points.point_003: Limited baseline comparisons without adapter. The paper omits comparisons to other continuous prompt tuning methods that do not require relation annotations, weakening claims of novelty
critique_points.point_004: Oracle model uses unrealistic gold relation labels at inference. The oracle MoE model relies on gold relation labels at inference, limiting practical relevance and no exploration of noisy relation prediction is provided
critique_points.point_005: Evaluation metrics and consistency definition are limited or ambiguous. Only Precision@1 is reported without metrics capturing partial correctness or semantic similarity; consistency metric lacks clarity on prompt pair selection
critique_points.point_006: Reproducibility details are insufficient. Hyperparameters for training P-Adapters and prompt preprocessing steps, especially for OOD prompts with typos, are not detailed
critique_points.point_007: No discussion on scaling to larger LLMs or multi-token object prediction. Experiments are limited to BERT and RoBERTa sizes; multi-token object prediction is not addressed, limiting generalizability and applicability
critique_points.point_008: No evaluation beyond LAMA or on language/domain variation. All experiments focus on LAMA relations in English, with no tests on other datasets, languages, or specialized domains
critique_points.point_009: Figures and tables lack statistical significance reporting. Reported improvements do not include confidence intervals or significance tests, making robustness of gains unclear
critique_points.point_010: Insufficient explanation of key mechanisms. The interpolation mechanism for subject embedding is not clearly explained or formalized, hindering understanding
